documents.title: JSR Score Optimization
documents.content: This document outlines the optimizations made to improve the JSR (JavaScript Registry) score for @signaler/cli.

documents.title: Overview
documents.content: The package structure has been optimized to achieve a high JSR score by addressing: 1. File Organization - Proper JSR best practices 2. Publication Configuration - Optimized include/exclude patterns 3. Bundle Size - Minimized while maintaining functionality 4. Type Safety - Complete TypeScript declarations 5. Documentation - Comprehensive API documentation

documents.title: 1. File Organization (JSR Best Practices)
documents.content: - Structured Exports : All exports in follow proper TypeScript patterns - Consistent Naming : Import and types files are co-located in same directories - Modular Architecture : Clear separation between CLI, API, core, runners, reporting, infrastructure, and UI modules

documents.title: JSR Configuration (`jsr.json`)
documents.content: - Selective Inclusion : Only essential files and documentation included - Comprehensive Exclusion : 44+ exclusion patterns to remove unnecessary files - Optimized Exports : 8 main export paths with proper TypeScript support

documents.title: NPM Configuration (`package.json`)
documents.content: - Minimal Files Array : Only core distribution files included - Build Optimization : Automated optimization in script

documents.title: NPM Ignore (`.npmignore`)
documents.content: - Source Exclusion : All source files, tests, and development artifacts excluded - Configuration Exclusion : Build configs, environment files, and development tools excluded - Documentation Filtering : Only essential documentation included

documents.title: Build Process Improvements
documents.content: - Comment Removal : in TypeScript build - Internal Stripping : to remove internal APIs - No Source Maps : Disabled for production builds - No Declaration Maps : Disabled to reduce file count

documents.title: Automated Cleanup
documents.content: - Build Optimization Script : removes unnecessary files - Empty Directory Removal : Cleans up empty directories after build - File Validation : Ensures all JavaScript files have corresponding TypeScript declarations

documents.title: Size Metrics
documents.content: - Current Bundle Size : 1.4MB (optimized from larger original) - File Count : 284 files (down from potentially 300+) - Type Safety : 100% TypeScript declaration coverage

documents.title: Validation Scripts
documents.content: - JSR Preparation : validates JSR requirements - Build Optimization : ensures optimal structure - Property Testing : Comprehensive property-based tests for publication optimization

documents.title: Automated Checks
documents.content: - Export Validation : All exports point to existing files - Type Declaration Coverage : Every JavaScript file has corresponding - File Size Monitoring : Warns about oversized files - Structure Validation : Ensures proper directory organization

documents.title: Known Limits
documents.content: This document tracks practical limits that users should account for in production workflows.

documents.title: Distribution and lifecycle
documents.content: - GitHub Release only — npm and JSR are deprecated; portable zip + / (or the Windows setup exe) is the supported path. - Shell matters on Windows — Git Bash uses ; PowerShell uses . Do not run in Bash. See install matrix. - First install is slow — expect 5–15 minutes while npm pulls Lighthouse, Playwright, and related tooling inside the portable bundle. - removes install files but not PATH entries added to your shell profile or Windows user PATH; clean those manually after uninstall. - on Window

documents.title: Lighthouse parity and scoring
documents.content: - Throughput mode is trend-focused and may score lower than manual DevTools Lighthouse runs. - Performance prioritization uses issue-count triage ( ), not category score parity. See Lab semantics. - Full-suite fidelity with very low parallelism can become slow and still drift in score quality. - Recommended policy: - Throughput for broad detection and trend comparison. - Fidelity on focused worst-N reruns for parity checks.

documents.title: Suite Size and Runtime
documents.content: - Large route/device suites can be CPU-heavy on mainstream hardware. - Auto resource profiles cap parallelism to reduce runaway load, but wall-clock can still grow significantly. - Keep route inventory intentional and avoid full-suite deep captures on every PR.

documents.title: Route Discovery Constraints
documents.content: - Dynamic routes may be excluded by default depending on scope/profile and detector rules. - Discovery quality depends on project structure and accurate project root/base URL configuration.

documents.title: Artifact and Token Budget Considerations
documents.content: - Default v3 artifacts are token-conscious, but legacy artifacts can become expensive. - Enabling increases output volume and downstream AI token usage. - For AI workflows, start from and follow pointers.

documents.title: Rust Accelerator Availability
documents.content: - Rust accelerators are opt-in and can fall back to Node when sidecar execution is unavailable. - Fallback is safe by design but does not guarantee speedup on every environment.

documents.content: Status: In Progress Date: March 23, 2026 Owners: Core CLI + Rust runtime Depends on: success-gate adoption completion

documents.title: 1. Summary
documents.content: V6.4 expands Signaler from Lighthouse-dominant lab analysis to a multi-source quality engine while preserving: 1. deterministic agent contracts, 2. local-first execution, 3. optional external inputs, 4. Rust-first performance on heavy data paths. The default workflow remains unchanged: 1. 2. 3. 4. 5.

documents.title: 2. Why This Matters
documents.content: Lighthouse remains a core benchmark, but it is not enough alone for production decision quality. High-value adjacent signals include: 1. field CWV (CrUX/RUM), 2. accessibility conformance depth (WCAG 2.2/APG), 3. security baseline coverage (OWASP Top 10/ASVS-lite), 4. technical SEO and structured-data integrity, 5. reliability and latency SLO context, 6. cross-browser parity signals.

documents.title: 3.1 In Scope
documents.content: 1. Add optional adapter contracts for non-Lighthouse signal families. 2. Merge accepted signals into ranking with explicit policy gates. 3. Emit additive metadata for comparability and source provenance. 4. Move heavy normalization/scoring paths to Rust with Node fallback.

documents.title: 3.2 Out of Scope
documents.content: 1. Replacing Lighthouse run engine. 2. Hosted SaaS monitoring backend. 3. Breaking schema changes for existing v3/v6 consumers.

documents.title: 4. Signal Families (Planned)
documents.content: 1. : CrUX/RUM p75 metrics and trend deltas. 2. : WCAG 2.2/APG-focused checks not covered in Lighthouse default categories. 3. : headers/cookies/TLS/config findings mapped to OWASP categories. 4. : indexability, canonical consistency, robots/sitemap/meta/schema checks. 5. : route-level availability/error/latency context. 6. : route/device/browser variance snapshots.

documents.title: 5. Contract Direction
documents.content: All additions remain additive: 1. Keep existing required fields in , , , . 2. Add optional metadata block in machine artifacts: - - - - - - - 3. Preserve explicit formula text and version marker in .

documents.title: 6. Ranking Policy (Draft)
documents.content: 1. Compute existing base priority as-is. 2. Apply bounded additive multipliers from accepted non-Lighthouse signals. 3. Clamp each source-family contribution and total composite boost. 4. Emit per-source contribution metadata for traceability. 5. Keep deterministic ordering on tied scores (stable sort by id/path/device).

documents.title: 7.1 Rust Modules
documents.content: 1. : - parse/validate external records - enforce freshness/confidence/pointer policy - emit normalized intermediate rows 2. : - route/device/issue grouping - bounded streaming reductions - deterministic source contribution summaries 3. : - composite score calculation - deterministic tie-break ordering - parity-tested against Node fallback

documents.title: 7.2 Node Responsibilities
documents.content: 1. CLI UX and command parsing 2. config and docs integration 3. contract emission and migration messaging 4. fallback path when Rust feature flags are disabled

documents.title: 8. Validation Plan
documents.content: 1. Contract tests: - valid/invalid fixtures per signal family - additive schema compatibility checks 2. Determinism tests: - identical input = identical ranking and digest 3. Policy tests: - stale/low-confidence/unmapped signals rejected and counted 4. Rust parity tests: - Rust vs Node output equivalence for normalization and ranking 5. Performance tests: - runtime and memory gates on medium/large fixture sets

documents.title: 9. Success Criteria
documents.content: 1. Multi-source path is opt-in and non-blocking by default. 2. Machine artifacts stay readable for agents within documented token budgets. 3. Composite ranking improves issue prioritization quality in dogfood repos. 4. Rust path provides measurable speedup on large multi-source fixtures. 5. No regressions in existing v3/v6 workflow semantics.

documents.title: 10. Rollout
documents.content: 1. Stage 1: fixture-only adapters + metadata scaffolding. 2. Stage 2: ranking integration under conservative policy defaults. 3. Stage 3: Rust normalizer/aggregator default-on with Node fallback. 4. Stage 4: release gate validation and public benchmark report. Execution detail and acceptance gates are tracked in: -

Primary V6 promise: - agents get high-signal action packets quickly - findings stay evidence-linked and reproducible - verification loops are explicit and automatable - runtime and token overhead remain bounded on mainstream hardware

documents.title: 5. Analyze/Verify Data Flow
documents.content: 1. writes canonical baseline artifacts. 2. ingests baseline and emits compact plus markdown digest. 3. selects actions/routes, executes scoped rerun, and emits deterministic . 4. CI/agents use as pass/fail automation signal.

documents.title: 6. Rust Core Plan (Decision Complete)
documents.content: Default runtime policy: - Rust sidecar enabled by default for hot paths in V6. - Node fallback is mandatory for every Rust path. - fallback reason must be emitted in command/runtime metadata. V6 Rust modules: 1. queue/scheduler execution planner 2. artifact reducer + ranking precompute 3. verify delta comparator Feature flags: - force-enable core sidecar in transitional releases - force-disable and run Node path only

documents.title: 7. CI and Release Gates
documents.content: New CI checks: - schema validation for and - deterministic ranking snapshot tests for - verify pass/fail behavior tests for threshold edge cases - fallback tests (Rust unavailable, malformed output, timeout) V6 gate: - block release if or schema or behavior tests fail - allow warn-only for manual dogfood evidence tracking

documents.title: 8. Test Cases and Scenarios
documents.content: Contract tests: 1. rejects missing canonical inputs under . 2. never emits empty-evidence actions. 3. rejects comparability mismatch when strict mode on. 4. returns exit code when checks fail. Behavior tests: 1. caps action/evidence counts. 2. writes plan and exits . 3. respects explicit action selection. 4. route selection is deterministic for same inputs. E2E tests: 1. succeeds on synthetic fixture in <= 10 minutes. 2. rerun failure still writes valid with failed status. 3. Node fallback path

documents.content: Phase rollout: 1. release and behind for one minor release 2. promote v6 contract to default in next release 3. keep v3 compatibility outputs for one additional release Migration mapping: - old agent entrypoint: - new primary decision packet: - old manual rerun loop: - new verification loop:

documents.title: 10. Assumptions and Defaults
documents.content: - package identity remains - canonical v5 commands remain available during v6 rollout - Rust sidecar remains optional at install-time, required only for acceleration - external adapters (PSI/CrUX/RUM/WPT) stay optional and non-blocking in initial V6
